2009-06-15_ucsd-faculty-statement-budget.context: In June 2009 California cut UC funding by roughly $813 million, prompting department chairs and senior faculty at UC San Diego to issue a collective statement, authored by sociologist Andrew Scull and co-signed by chairs across the sciences, engineering, arts, and social sciences. It warned UC leadership that the budget plans for 2009–10 and beyond would reduce the university to 'a mediocre shell' of its former self, and floated controversial remedies including concentrating resources on the research-intensive campuses.
2009-06-15_ucsd-faculty-statement-budget.outcome: President Mark Yudof rejected the proposal to shift resources among campuses; on July 16, 2009 the Regents declared a financial emergency and approved a systemwide furlough plan 20–1.

links.label: Full text — Remaking the University archive
UCSF News — Regents approve furlough plan (July 16, 2009)

2009-06-29_ucb-faculty-oppose-salary-cut.context: As President Yudof circulated three options for systemwide salary cuts and furloughs to meet the $813 million shortfall, 141 UC Berkeley faculty wrote to UCOP stating they could support none of them. The letter cited insufficient financial transparency, inadequate consultation time, and the pairing of salary cuts with student fee increases, which signers argued imperiled UC's identity as an accessible public institution.
2009-06-29_ucb-faculty-oppose-salary-cut.outcome: Yudof revised the proposal after more than 3,000 comments from UC employees; on July 16, 2009 the Regents approved a graduated furlough plan (4–10% salary reductions) effective September 1.

links.label: Full text — Remaking the University archive
Berkeley News — Regents approve furlough plan

2009-07-03_ucsb-save-uc-letter.context: At the depth of the 2009 budget crisis, 134 faculty — primarily from UC Santa Barbara — addressed all ten UC chancellors, asking them to make a strong, unified public case to the Legislature and Regents for the university's importance to California's economy and workforce. The letter argued the state was choosing to divest from public higher education just as the economy demanded more graduates, not fewer.
2009-07-03_ucsb-save-uc-letter.outcome: No unified chancellors' statement followed; the Regents proceeded with furloughs in July and a 32% student fee increase that November.

links.label: Full text — Remaking the University archive

2009-07-09_ucla-sociology-statement-budg.context: The UCLA Sociology Department issued a collective statement on the contraction of the university's budget, signed by 18 of its faculty, raising concerns about the effects of furloughs, hiring freezes, and course reductions on research capacity and graduate training. It came as UCLA's general-fund cut deepened to roughly $132 million (about 17%) for 2009–10, and was part of a wave of departmental statements across the system.

links.label: Remaking the University archive (UC budget-crisis statements)
UCLA Chancellor — 2009 budget update

2009-07-12_lakoff-letter-to-regents-endo.context: UC Berkeley cognitive scientist George Lakoff circulated an open letter to the Board of Regents calling for protection of the university's public mission and of faculty shared governance during the budget emergency, and arguing that salary cuts and furloughs should not be coupled with student fee increases. Faculty across the system were invited to endorse it; 1,151 had signed by the logged date, making it the largest faculty sign-on of the 2009 crisis.
2009-07-12_lakoff-letter-to-regents-endo.outcome: The Regents approved the furlough plan on July 16, 2009 and a 32% fee increase in November; the letter's endorsement list became a key artifact of systemwide faculty sentiment.

links.label: Lakoff letter & endorsements — Remaking the University archive

2009-11-22_ucb-faculty-letter-birgeneau-.context: On November 20, 2009, students occupied Wheeler Hall at Berkeley to protest the Regents' 32% fee increase; police from multiple agencies responded with 46 arrests and violent confrontations with crowds outside the building. Two days later 127 Berkeley faculty — Judith Butler among them — wrote to Chancellor Robert Birgeneau condemning what they called 'unwarranted and illegal police brutality,' rejecting his framing of the events, and demanding an independent investigation.
2009-11-22_ucb-faculty-letter-birgeneau-.outcome: Birgeneau ordered an independent review; the Police Review Board's June 2010 report (the Brazil report) faulted police and administrative preparation and communication, findings the chancellor called 'sobering.'

links.label: Verso — Judith Butler signs open letter to Birgeneau
Berkeley News — Police Review Board report (June 2010)

2009-12-04_ucla-faculty-open-letter-bloc.context: During the November 18–19, 2009 Regents meeting at UCLA, where the 32% fee increase was approved, police used Tasers and batons on protesters outside Covel Commons. UCLA faculty responded with an open letter to Chancellor Gene Block (published in the Daily Bruin as 'A failure to keep the peace,' with 119 names in the published version) expressing grave concern over the use of force and calling for an independent investigation of police conduct and a review of campus protest policies.
2009-12-04_ucla-faculty-open-letter-bloc.outcome: Block convened a town hall and ordered a review; the resulting panel found police conduct largely within policy while urging better communication to reduce future use of force.

links.label: Daily Bruin — 'A failure to keep the peace' (Dec 6, 2009)
UCLA Chancellor — review of police response

2010-03-01_uci-faculty-support-march-4th.context: The March 4, 2010 Statewide Day of Action to Defend Public Education — called at an October 2009 Berkeley organizing conference — mobilized UC, CSU, community college, and K-12 campuses against budget cuts, fee increases, and layoffs. In the run-up, 46 UC Irvine faculty signed a statement of support framing the action as a defense of public higher education in California.
2010-03-01_uci-faculty-support-march-4th.outcome: March 4 demonstrations occurred statewide, including a complete shutdown of UC Santa Cruz and rallies of thousands in Los Angeles and San Francisco; the broader budget trajectory did not change.

links.label: Labor Notes — March 4 Day of Action
New University (UCI) — March 4 coverage

2010-04-20_ucb-faculty-petition-osc-char.context: After the November 2009 Wheeler Hall occupation, Berkeley's Office of Student Conduct opened disciplinary cases against many protesters. In April 2010, 131 Berkeley faculty petitioned Chancellor Birgeneau to suspend all OSC proceedings until what they described as serious procedural deficiencies — weak evidentiary standards, guilt-by-association charging, and targeting of political activity — were remedied.
2010-04-20_ucb-faculty-petition-osc-char.outcome: Proceedings continued but with mixed results: hearing panels acquitted at least one Wheeler occupier, and later cases ended in acquittals or negotiated probation rather than suspension.

links.label: reclaim UC — faculty petition text (April 2010)

2011-02-09_uci-faculty-drop-charges.context: After eleven Muslim Student Union members repeatedly disrupted Israeli Ambassador Michael Oren's February 2010 talk at UC Irvine, the university disciplined the students and suspended the MSU — and a year later the Orange County District Attorney filed criminal misdemeanor charges. Exactly 100 UCI faculty, including five deans (among them law dean Erwin Chemerinsky), urged DA Tony Rackauckas to drop the charges, arguing the conduct was wrong but that campus discipline had sufficed and criminal prosecution of nonviolent protest set a dangerous precedent.
2011-02-09_uci-faculty-drop-charges.outcome: The DA proceeded; in September 2011 a jury convicted ten defendants of both misdemeanors, with sentences of community service and probation. The convictions were upheld on appeal.

links.label: UCI News — 100 faculty call on D.A. to drop charges
Inside Higher Ed — Irvine 11 found guilty
Wikipedia — Irvine 11 controversy

2011-11-30_katehi-has-faculty-support.context: On November 18, 2011, a UC Davis police officer pepper-sprayed seated, nonviolent demonstrators at an Occupy encampment on the quad, igniting national calls for Chancellor Linda Katehi's resignation. In response, 206 Davis faculty (organized by entomologist Walter Leal) published a letter in the Davis Enterprise condemning the pepper-spraying itself while defending Katehi as the right leader for the campus's recovery and calling resignation demands premature — a counterpoint to the English department and Faculty Association votes demanding she step down.
2011-11-30_katehi-has-faculty-support.outcome: Katehi stayed; in spring 2012 the Davis Academic Senate rejected a no-confidence motion 697–312 while censuring aspects of the administration's conduct. The officer was dismissed in July 2012; Katehi ultimately resigned the chancellorship in 2016 amid separate controversies.

links.label: Davis Enterprise — 'Katehi has faculty support'
Wikipedia — UC Davis pepper spray incident

2015-02-25_ucla-daily-bruin-diversity-re.context: UCLA's College of Letters and Science spent 2014–15 debating a proposed undergraduate diversity course requirement; a November 2014 College vote approved it narrowly (332–303), and opponents petitioned for a broader ballot. Thirty-six professors published this Daily Bruin submission urging every faculty member to weigh arguments on both sides before the campus-wide vote, contending that support so far was thin, that the official ballot materials had been one-sided, and that so large a curricular change required an unambiguous mandate.
2015-02-25_ucla-daily-bruin-diversity-re.outcome: In the campus-wide mail ballot (March 30 – April 10, 2015), faculty approved the requirement 916–487; it took effect for students entering the College in fall 2017.

links.label: Daily Bruin — the faculty submission (Feb 25, 2015)
UCLA Newsroom — faculty approves diversity requirement
Inside Higher Ed — UCLA approves diversity requirement

2020-02-10_ucsc-faculty-oppose-class-dis.context: During the UCSC graduate students' wildcat COLA strike (December 2019 – March 2020), the administration circulated a 'Notification of Class and Section Disruption' form inviting undergraduates to report teaching disruptions. Two hundred sixteen UCSC faculty signed this statement opposing the form, arguing it invited surveillance of classrooms, chilled academic freedom, deepened campus divisions, and undermined the university's mission.
2020-02-10_ucsc-faculty-oppose-class-dis.outcome: On February 28, 2020 UCSC dismissed roughly 54 striking graduate employees for withholding grades; most were reinstated under an August 2020 settlement that added a housing stipend, though the demanded COLA was not granted.

links.label: Academe Blog — 200+ UCSC faculty respond (Feb 10, 2020)
Wikipedia — 2020 Santa Cruz graduate students' strike

2021-12_uc-faculty-replace-ca-math-frame.context: California's draft 2021–22 Mathematics Framework proposed 'detracking' students through 10th grade (ending routine 8th-grade Algebra I), promoted data-science pathways as alternatives to the calculus track, and wove social-justice themes through instruction. This open letter, hosted by the Independent Institute from July 13, 2021, called for the draft to be replaced outright, arguing it would limit access to advanced mathematics, disadvantage public-school students relative to private and international peers, and fall hardest on lower-income families. It grew past 1,200 signers — roughly 410 of them UC faculty.
2021-12_uc-faculty-replace-ca-math-frame.outcome: The State Board of Education adopted a heavily revised framework on July 12, 2023: detracking language was softened to preserve 8th-grade algebra acceleration, and data science was dropped as an Algebra II substitute after BOARS' own July 2023 intervention.

links.label: Independent Institute — the open letter
Independent Institute — 1,160+ signers news release (Nov 2021)
EdWeek — California adopts revised math framework (July 2023)

uc-a-g-ethnic-studies-support.context: With BOARS weighing course criteria for a proposed ethnic studies A-G admission requirement (approved in principle in 2020), this sign-on statement defended the proposal and the ethnic-studies scholars who drafted its criteria. It affirmed ethnic studies as a rigorous, half-century-old field, tied the requirement to AB 101 (which made ethnic studies a high-school graduation requirement) and to CSU's parallel mandate, and objected to what it called outside intervention in the Senate's deliberative process. It drew 1,349 individual signatories plus organizational endorsements; this corpus tracks the 593 matched to UC faculty rosters.
uc-a-g-ethnic-studies-support.outcome: BOARS declined to advance the criteria in June 2022; revised criteria passed BOARS in June 2023 (9–2) but were voted down 6–5–1 that November, and the systemwide review the Academic Council launched in January 2024 had still not produced a final Assembly vote as of 2025.

links.label: Statement text — UCSC Critical Race & Ethnic Studies
EdSource — debate over UC ethnic studies criteria
Inside Higher Ed — requirement waylaid amid war (Jan 2024)

2022-05-31_uc-faculty-letter-boars-ethni.context: The counterpart to the support statement: UCLA law professor Richard Sander's May 4, 2022 letter of concern to BOARS was circulated for wider UC faculty signature (with a May 31 deadline) by the California Association of Scholars. Signers argued the draft Area H criteria were ideologically prescriptive, departed from UC's historically content-neutral A-G standards, and risked imposing one contested vision of ethnic studies on every California high school.
2022-05-31_uc-faculty-letter-boars-ethni.outcome: BOARS voted in June 2022 not to advance the criteria — an outcome the letter's organizers claimed as a victory. The proposal's later revival (approved June 2023, rejected November 2023, sent to systemwide review January 2024) remained unresolved as of 2025.

links.label: Minding the Campus — the circulated letter (May 31, 2022)
EdSource — debate over UC ethnic studies criteria

2022-11-23_uc-faculty-pledge-solidarity-.context: Nine days into the November 2022 strike by 48,000 UC academic workers (UAW 2865, 5810, SRU-UAW) — the largest academic strike in U.S. history — Senate faculty organized by Simeon Man (UCSD), Annie McClanahan (UCI), Joshua Clover (UCD) and others launched this pledge. Its 436 signers committed to honor picket lines by withholding instructional labor — teaching, advising, grade submission — for the strike's duration.
2022-11-23_uc-faculty-pledge-solidarity-.outcome: The UAW units reached tentative agreements between December 16 and 23, 2022, ending the strike with large multi-year raises, childcare subsidies, and expanded protections.

links.label: UC Faculty Pledge of Solidarity — official site
EdSource — hundreds of UC faculty pledge support
Wikipedia — 2022 UC academic workers' strike

2023_uc-faculty-open-letter-k12-mathemat.context: Organized by Boaz Barak (Harvard), Edith Cohen (Google), Adrian Mims (The Calculus Project), and Jelani Nelson (UC Berkeley), the k12mathmatters open letter responded to the same draft California Mathematics Framework as the Independent Institute letter, from a deliberately cross-ideological STEM coalition. It warned against detracking that would curtail 8th-grade algebra and against promoting data-science courses as substitutes for the algebra-to-calculus pathway, arguing both would widen gaps for under-resourced students. Signature gathering was restricted to U.S.-based STEM professionals; the list reached 1,786 by mid-2022, including the 157 UC faculty tracked here. (The corpus slug says '2023'; the letter itself launched December 3, 2021.)
2023_uc-faculty-open-letter-k12-mathemat.outcome: The framework adopted July 12, 2023 retreated on both points the letter targeted: it preserved acceleration options and dropped data science as an Algebra II substitute.

links.label: k12mathmatters — the open letter
Scott Aaronson — launch cross-post (Dec 3, 2021)
EdWeek — California adopts revised framework

2024-05-01_no-police-actions-ucla.context: UCLA's Palestine Solidarity Encampment, established April 25, 2024 on Royce Quad, was attacked on the night of April 30–May 1 by counter-protesters using fireworks, chemical sprays, and blunt weapons while security failed to intervene for hours. Launched in the immediate aftermath, this letter to Chancellor Gene Block demanded that no police action be taken against the encampment, rejected characterizations of the encampment as inherently antisemitic, and held the administration responsible for student safety. It drew 901 signatures from faculty and staff.
2024-05-01_no-police-actions-ucla.outcome: The letter did not avert the sweep: on the night of May 1–2 police cleared the encampment and arrested more than 200 people. UCLA Academic Senate votes to censure (50%) and declare no confidence in (43%) Block later failed narrowly.

links.label: Wikipedia — 2024 UCLA pro-Palestinian campus occupation
CNN — UCLA faculty reject censure and no-confidence (May 18, 2024)

2024-05-06_uc-faculty-staff-demand-ucla.context: After the May 2 police clearance of the UCLA encampment, faculty and staff from across the ten campuses signed this systemwide demand letter. Its demands included Chancellor Block's resignation, amnesty and dropped charges for arrested students, faculty, and staff, disclosure of and divestment from weapons-linked investments, an independent investigation of UCPD, LAPD, and CHP conduct, and support funds for those injured. About 800 signed within the first 48 hours; 906 are recorded here.
2024-05-06_uc-faculty-staff-demand-ucla.outcome: Block did not resign (he stepped down at his term's end in summer 2024, and most protester charges were not pursued); the demands framed much of the faculty mobilization that followed, including the UAW 4811 strike.

links.label: Daily Bruin — faculty call for divestment, amnesty (May 9, 2024)
CBS LA — faculty demand chancellor's resignation

2024-05-08_uc-faculty-for-integrity-lett.context: The principal counterpoint to the pro-encampment letters: 512 UC faculty wrote to the Board of Regents arguing that antisemitism had reached crisis levels on UC campuses and that faculty and academic departments were misusing university resources to advance an academic boycott of Israel, of which they saw the encampments as an expression. The letter urged the Regents to adopt and enforce robust discipline for policy violations and pressed for restrictions on political statements posted on official departmental sites.
2024-05-08_uc-faculty-for-integrity-lett.outcome: In July 2024 the Regents adopted a policy restricting political statements on departmental homepages — a measure aligned with the letter's aims. The letter remained a touchstone in later antisemitism-related federal scrutiny of UC.

links.label: Letter text (PDF, UCLA mirror)
Jewish Journal — 500+ UC faculty call on Regents

2024-05-10_ucla-sociology-response-gaza-.context: Released in the wave of UCLA departmental statements that followed the encampment's clearance (the archived roster is dated May 10, 2024), the Sociology department's response condemned the administration's 'failures' — first failing to protect encamped students from the April 30 mob attack, then enabling the May 2 police operation against them. Addressed to President Drake, Chancellor Block, and the Regents' chair, it accumulated 514 signatures through a rolling sign-on that reached far beyond the department.
2024-05-10_ucla-sociology-response-gaza-.outcome: It joined statements from more than a dozen UCLA departments in the record before the Academic Senate's mid-May censure and no-confidence debates, both of which narrowly failed.

links.label: Daily Bruin — departments release statements (May 4, 2024)
Statement site (archived)

2024-05-13_ucsd-faculty-testimony-gaza-e.context: A two-part eyewitness document: 86 UCSD professors who had personally visited the campus Gaza solidarity encampment described it as peaceful — with teach-ins, Shabbat services, and Muslim prayers — and rejected claims that it was antisemitic or obstructive, joined by 160 colleagues endorsing their testimony (244 signatures recorded here). It responded to Chancellor Pradeep Khosla's May 6 decision to clear the encampment with multi-agency riot police and 64 arrests.
2024-05-13_ucsd-faculty-testimony-gaza-e.outcome: Faculty attempting to deliver this and a related petition to Khosla's office on May 16 found it locked and slid the letters under the door; the testimony fed into the June 4 Representative Assembly's competing confidence resolutions.

links.label: The Triton — faculty attempt to deliver petitions
UCSD Guardian — faculty try to deliver statements

2024-05-27_ucsd-senate-no-confidence-kho.context: This sign-on resolution of no confidence in Chancellor Khosla, submitted for the San Diego Division's June 4, 2024 Representative Assembly, charged that his May 6 summoning of UCPD, SDPD, and CHP against a peaceful encampment was an unjustified escalation that violated free expression, ignored available mediation (campus police liaisons, the Ombuds office), and bypassed shared governance; it also cited his weeks of public silence. One hundred Senate faculty had signed as of May 27.
2024-05-27_ucsd-senate-no-confidence-kho.outcome: The June 4 Assembly sent the competing questions to an electronic ballot of all Division members (distributed ~June 11); no public tally was announced, and Khosla remained in office. Student government bodies had separately passed no-confidence resolutions.

links.label: Resolution text (item 14d, PDF)
June 4, 2024 Representative Assembly notice (PDF)
San Diego Faculty Association — endorsement

2024-05_ucsd-senate-confidence-khosla.context: The counter-resolution: 211 UCSD Senate faculty endorsed a vote of confidence in Chancellor Khosla, arguing his May 6 removal of the unauthorized encampment protected campus safety and access, balanced free speech against Title VI obligations, and responded proportionately to an encampment that had tripled in size in violation of law and policy. It reached the same June 4, 2024 Representative Assembly as the no-confidence resolution.
2024-05_ucsd-senate-confidence-khosla.outcome: Both questions went to the Division-wide electronic ballot in June 2024; no public tally was released, and Khosla continued as chancellor.

links.label: Resolution text (item 14a, PDF)
June 4, 2024 Representative Assembly notice (PDF)

2024-05_uc-faculty-pledge-non-retaliatio.context: As UAW 4811 — the 48,000-member union of UC academic workers — moved toward an unfair-labor-practice strike over the university's handling of the encampment protests, faculty circulated this pledge committing not to retaliate against union members who honored a lawful ULP strike (in grading, evaluations, letters, or appointments). Faculty associations publicized it ahead of the May 13–15 strike-authorization vote; 991 faculty signed.
2024-05_uc-faculty-pledge-non-retaliatio.outcome: The authorization passed May 15; stand-up strikes began at UCSC May 20 and spread to UCLA and Davis before a June 7 court order halted them. The pledge stood as a faculty-side solidarity commitment through the litigation.

links.label: UCSB Faculty Association — pledge invitation (May 9, 2024)
Pledge text (Google Doc)

2024-05_ucla-jewish-faculty-staff-open-l.context: Written by UCLA Jewish faculty and staff who rejected the administration's invocation of Jewish student safety to justify clearing the encampment, this open letter called for amnesty for arrested protesters, asked Chancellor Block to retract his attribution of violence to the encampment, insisted that criticism of Israel is not presumptively antisemitic, and noted that its signers spanned anti-Zionist to Zionist commitments. Sign-ons (76 recorded here) began May 5, 2024 per the form's response log.
2024-05_ucla-jewish-faculty-staff-open-l.outcome: The letter entered the Congressional record around Block's May 23 testimony and was re-circulated in 2026 — growing to 132 signatories — as counter-evidence in the federal antisemitism litigation over UCLA, where the Jewish Faculty Resilience Group took the opposing view.

links.label: Letter and signatories (Google Sites)
Submitted to Congress (PDF)
The Forward — dueling Jewish faculty letters (2026)

2024_ucla-math-dept-statement-administra.context: Thirty-three members of the UCLA Mathematics Department signed this statement condemning the administration's conduct around the encampment: the failure to protect students during the April 30 mob attack — which it called a concerted act of political violence without analog on the protesters' side — and the decision barely 24 hours later to clear the encampment by force, with rubber bullets, stun grenades, and mass arrests. It explicitly framed the critique as independent of signers' political views on the underlying conflict.
2024_ucla-math-dept-statement-administra.outcome: It joined the cluster of departmental statements (indexed under 'We Stand With Our Students') that preceded the UCLA Senate's narrowly failed censure and no-confidence votes.

links.label: 'We Stand With Our Students' index of UCLA statements
Daily Bruin — departments release statements

2025-04-15_berkeley-division-petition-sp.context: Forty-five Berkeley Senate faculty petitioned to convene a rare Special Meeting of the Berkeley Division in response to federal actions against universities — funding threats, investigations, and data demands. The April 22, 2025 Zoom meeting, organized with the Berkeley Faculty Association and the Berkeley Initiative for Free Inquiry, considered a Resolution to Protect Academic and Political Freedom urging UC to reject and resist unlawful federal demands and protect community members' data.
2025-04-15_berkeley-division-petition-sp.outcome: Roughly 95% of the ~450 faculty attending approved the resolution, among the first formal divisional declarations against the 2025 federal pressure campaign.

links.label: Berkeley Academic Senate — special meeting page
Petition (PDF)
Daily Cal — division urges UC to resist federal attacks

2025-06-20_uc-faculty-letter-drake-trell.context: Against UC's systemwide mandate to install Trellix endpoint-detection software on computers used for university work — including personal and research machines — 988 faculty wrote to President Michael Drake objecting that the software's root-level access enabled surveillance, extraction, and alteration of files without consent, threatening research confidentiality, attorney-client and medical data, and academic freedom. The letter demanded a halt to implementation and genuine consultation with the Academic Senate, following the systemwide Representative Assembly's June 12 resolution (82% supermajority) to the same effect.
2025-06-20_uc-faculty-letter-drake-trell.outcome: Drake did not halt the mandate; enforcement proceeded into the summer and the dispute carried over to his successor.

links.label: Letter text (CUCFA, PDF)
Science — UC faculty push back against 'Big Brother' cybersecurity mandate
Systemwide Senate resolution on Trellix (June 2025)

2025-08-01_uc-faculty-letter-milliken-tr.context: On James B. Milliken's first day as UC President, 630 faculty asked him to revisit the Trellix mandate his predecessor had declined to pause, reiterating concerns about root-level access to machines holding sensitive research, health, and politically exposed data, and noting that an 82% Senate supermajority resolution had been disregarded. Organizers highlighted scope creep — UC Irvine had begun conditioning Canvas, VPN, and administrative access on installation.
2025-08-01_uc-faculty-letter-milliken-tr.outcome: No formal reversal followed; the AAUP lodged its own objection that October while faculty pursued public-records requests and campus-level accommodations, leaving the mandate in force but contested.

links.label: Letter text (PDF)
UCSB Faculty Association — Trellix update (July 2025)
Daily Bruin — faculty concerned over privacy (July 2025)

2025_delay-cybersecurity-mandate-petitio.context: The Council of UC Faculty Associations hosted this petition asking UCOP to delay the Trellix endpoint-security mandate until transparent use policies — covering capabilities, permissible uses, and abuse prevention — could be negotiated. It gathered 1,540 signatures between April 29 and September 12, 2025 (per the form's response log), making it the largest sign-on in this corpus, and ran alongside Senate resolutions at six campuses and the Drake and Milliken letters. The petition itself is tracked here as a roster; its substantive text lives in the two companion letters.
2025_delay-cybersecurity-mandate-petitio.outcome: UCOP granted no formal delay; the petition served as the organizing spine of the broader Trellix opposition campaign.

links.label: CUCFA — petition page
Summary of concerns (organizers' site)
Daily Cal — 'single point of failure'

2025_jewish-uc-faculty-letter-to-regents.context: As reports emerged that UC was negotiating a settlement of the Trump administration's 'systematic antisemitism' claims against UCLA, 235 Jewish UC faculty (as of late September 2025) wrote to the Regents urging them to refuse concessions, arguing that the federal campaign used antisemitism as a pretext to attack universities, and that capitulation would protect neither Jewish students nor the institution. Berkeley Law dean Erwin Chemerinsky was among the signers; the letter took the opposite position from the Jewish Faculty Resilience Group, which welcomed federal scrutiny.
2025_jewish-uc-faculty-letter-to-regents.outcome: No settlement on the administration's terms was announced in 2025; a federal judge separately ordered roughly $500 million in UCLA grants restored that September while litigation continued.

links.label: Letter text (Google Doc)
J. — 'Don't negotiate with Trump over antisemitism claims' (Sept 24, 2025)

2025_jews-in-defense-of-uc.context: Days after the Trump administration demanded UCLA pay a $1 billion fine (plus a $172 million claims fund) to restore suspended research grants — a demand framed as antisemitism enforcement — Jewish members of the UC community published this letter declaring that 'cutting off hundreds of millions of research funds will do nothing to make UCLA safer for Jews nor diminish antisemitism in the world.' Organized by UCLA faculty spanning a wide range of views on Israel and opened for ongoing signature, it grew past 600 signers within a month; 645 are recorded here.
2025_jews-in-defense-of-uc.outcome: In September 2025 a federal judge ordered roughly $500 million in UCLA health-science grants restored; the $1 billion demand remained in litigation through the year's end.

links.label: Letter site — Jews in Defense of UC
UCLA Life Sciences — announcement (Aug 19, 2025)
Daily Bruin — Jewish researchers criticize funding cuts (Sept 2025)

2026-05-25_uc-stem-faculty-sat-act-lette.context: Led by UC Berkeley mathematician Zvezdelina Stankova, more than 1,290 UC STEM faculty asked the university to reinstate SAT/ACT math scores for STEM applicants beginning fall 2027 and to give STEM faculty formal oversight of readiness standards in their majors. The letter cites steep declines in calculus readiness since UC went test-free in 2020 — including a roughly thirty-fold rise in entering UCSD students testing below high-school math level — and was signed by 360+ mathematics faculty, seven of nine math department chairs, and 43+ STEM chairs systemwide. It is the largest faculty letter on admissions policy in UC history and the seed document of this corpus.
2026-05-25_uc-stem-faculty-sat-act-lette.outcome: As of early June 2026 no policy change had been announced: BOARS met June 5, 2026, signaled tentative interest in test requirements (Smarter Balanced for Californians, SAT/ACT for nonresidents), and owed a policy roadmap by June 30; any change requires Regents approval.

links.label: ucstudentsuccess.org — the letter and roster
Inside Higher Ed — hundreds of math professors ask UC to bring back SAT/ACT
NPR — interview with Zvezdelina Stankova (June 6, 2026)
